abstract:
Maintaining water quality is essential for human health, aquatic biodiversity, and industrial sustainability. In recent decades, increasing levels of environmental pollution have necessitated the development of advanced monitoring systems. This study addresses the challenge of accurately evaluating the Water Quality Index (WQI) and Water Quality Classification (WQC) through optimized machine learning frameworks. Using a dataset of 1,991 instances featuring seven key parameters—including pH, dissolved oxygen, and biological oxygen demand—the research employs rigorous preprocessing steps such as mean imputation and data normalization.

To enhance predictive performance, a grid search algorithm was integrated with cross-validation for hyperparameter tuning across eight different models. For the classification of water quality status, four ensemble methods were evaluated: Random Forest (RF), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), Gradient Boosting (GB), and Adaptive Boosting (AdaBoost). For the regression-based estimation of WQI values, four techniques were tested: K-nearest neighbors (KNN), decision trees (DT), support vector regression (SVR), and multi-layer perceptron (MLP) networks. The models were validated using a comprehensive suite of metrics, including accuracy, F1 score, and Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) for classification, alongside Mean Square Error (MSE) and R-squared (R²) for regression.

Experimental results demonstrate that the optimized Gradient Boosting model achieved the highest classification performance with an accuracy of 99.50%. In the regression analysis, the MLP regressor significantly outperformed other methods, attaining an R² value of 99.8%. These findings suggest that the integration of grid search optimization with ensemble and neural network architectures provides a highly reliable and efficient approach for real-time water quality assessment and environmental management.
